coredata.dc:title: Green-synthesized carbon dots from ginger: Multifunctional agents against oral pathogens with biocompatibility in human gingival fibroblast cells

coredata.dc:description: Persistent antibiotic use in treating oral infections often leads to drug resistance in pathogenic bacteria, notably impacting conditions like periodontitis. Addressing this challenge, the study pioneers the use of carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from ginger rhizomes (Zingiber officinale) as a novel biocompatible material. CDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method, emphasizing a green approach, and comprehensively characterized for their optical properties and structural uniformity. The synthesized CDs showed a zeta potential of −24.9 mV, confirming the formation of stable and well-dispersed particles. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) confirmed an average particle size of 2.9 nm, thus validating the formation of CDs. Biomedical assessments demonstrated that the synthesized CDs were non-cytotoxic to human gingival fibroblast cell lines, with effective free radical scavenging activity and high total antioxidant capacity, as indicated by their IC50 values. CDs also exhibited moderate inhibition of protein denaturation compared to the standard. Moreover, they showed significant inhibitory effects against bacterial strains (Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Lactobacillus acidophilus, Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus) and fungal strains (Aspergillus niger, Candida albicans) at minimal concentrations. Notably, CDs inhibited the growth of periodontal pathogens including Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans, Tannerella forsythia, Porphyromonas gingivalis, and Prevotella intermedia. These findings underscore the potential of CDs as multifunctional agents possessing anti-inflammatory, antifungal, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties. Remarkably, they offer a promising alternative to conventional antibiotics, potentially revolutionizing oral healthcare. Their proven biocompatibility and potent bioactivity underscore their innovative potential in biomedical research. Future studies should further assess their efficacy in vivo to fully harness their clinical potential.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Persistent antibiotic use in treating oral infections often leads to drug resistance in pathogenic bacteria, notably impacting conditions like periodontitis. Addressing this challenge, the study pioneers the use of carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from ginger rhizomes (Zingiber officinale) as a novel biocompatible material. CDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method, emphasizing a green approach, and comprehensively characterized for their optical properties and structural uniformity. The synthesized CDs showed a zeta potential of −24.9 mV, confirming the formation of stable and well-dispersed particles. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) confirmed an average particle size of 2.9 nm, thus validating the formation of CDs. Biomedical assessments demonstrated that the synthesized CDs were non-cytotoxic to human gingival fibroblast cell lines, with effective free radical scavenging activity and high total antioxidant capacity, as indicated by their IC50 values. CDs also exhibited moderate inhibition of protein denaturation compared to the standard. Moreover, they showed significant inhibitory effects against bacterial strains (Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Lactobacillus acidophilus, Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus) and fungal strains (Aspergillus niger, Candida albicans) at minimal concentrations. Notably, CDs inhibited the growth of periodontal pathogens including Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans, Tannerella forsythia, Porphyromonas gingivalis, and Prevotella intermedia. These findings underscore the potential of CDs as multifunctional agents possessing anti-inflammatory, antifungal, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties. Remarkably, they offer a promising alternative to conventional antibiotics, potentially revolutionizing oral healthcare. Their proven biocompatibility and potent bioactivity underscore their innovative potential in biomedical research. Future studies should further assess their efficacy in vivo to fully harness their clinical potential.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The oral cavity is a vital interface between the human body and the external environment [1]. The oral cavity, with its diverse and dynamic microbial community known as the oral microbiome, serves as the host to distinct microbial compositions across various sites. These compositions are intricately regulated by signaling mechanisms involving both host factors and external environmental influences, contributing significantly to the maintenance of oral health [1,2]. This microbiome includes bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protozoa that coexist in a delicate balance [3]. Disruption of this balance can lead to common oral infectious diseases such as dental caries, periodontitis, peri-implantitis, endodontic infections, orthodontic infections, and oral candidiasis [4]. If not promptly and accurately treated, these microbial infections can progressively worse, leading to irreversible damage to teeth and other oral tissues. Furthermore, chronic oral infections have been linked to an increased risk of systemic conditions, including oral cancer, due to disturbances in the host’s immune-inflammatory responses. Therefore, timely and effective treatment is essential to prevent complications and maintain oral health [5].

Recent evidence suggests that oral bacteria significantly influence systemic disorders such as coronary artery disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and gastrointestinal neoplasms [1,6,7]. This connection underscores the importance of timely and effective treatment of oral infections to prevent both local and systemic complications.

Presently available treatments for preserving oral health, including antibiotics, antimicrobial mouthwashes, and herbal products, exhibit efficacy but are not without limitations [8]. Antibiotics, for instance, bear the risk of fostering antibiotic-resistant bacterial strains, thus complicating long-term management strategies [9]. Similarly, antimicrobial mouthwashes have the potential to disrupt the delicate equilibrium of oral microbiota, potentially precipitating dysbiosis and subsequent oral health issues [10]. Herbal products, while often touted for their natural origins, may lack standardized formulations and rigorous scientific evidence regarding their efficacy and safety profiles [11]. This underscores the imperative for innovative therapeutic modalities capable of fostering sustained oral health.

As nanomedicine progresses, various nanomaterials have emerged as potent antibacterial agents capable of treating diverse infectious diseases caused by bacteria, viruses, and fungi [12,13]. These materials include antimicrobial peptides, inorganic nanoparticles, and natural drug agents. Among these, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant interest due to their unique properties, such as small size, high surface area, excellent photostability, good water solubility, and ease of functionalization [14]. CDs, particularly those synthesized through green methods, have shown promising potential in biomedical applications due to their minimal toxicity and environmental friendliness [15].

In recent years, various physical methods have been employed for synthesizing CDs, including chemical ablation, electrochemical carbonization, laser ablation, microwave irradiation, and hydrothermal treatments [16]. However, these techniques often exhibit significant drawbacks, such as the use of harsh chemicals, high energy consumption, intricate procedures, and potential environmental hazards, thereby limiting their practicality and scalability [17]. Consequently, there is a compelling need for more sustainable, cost-effective, and environmentally benign approaches that address these limitations while preserving or enhancing the efficacy and biocompatibility of the resulting carbon dots.

The green synthesis of CDs involves using natural precursors and environmentally benign methods, such as hydrothermal treatment, which not only reduces potential toxicity but also enhances the biocompatibility of the CDs [18,19]. A medicinal plant serves as an exemplary natural resource for the development of CDs. The inherent chemical composition and bioactive compounds of the precursor impart desirable properties to the CDs, frequently obviating the need for supplementary surface passivation [20].

Hitherto, various natural materials have been explored for the green synthesis of CDs, including fruits, vegetables, leaves, and even food wastes. Examples include muskmelon fruit, tomato, rose flower, Purslane leaves, Prawn shells, Sugarcane bagasse, orange peel, lemon peel, Rice husk, banana peel etc. each chosen for their abundant organic content and ease of availability [15,21]. Tea leaves, coffee grounds, and various herbs have also been utilized, owing to their rich polyphenol and flavonoid content which can impart beneficial properties to the synthesized CDs [20]. Thus, the utilization of natural resources in the development of CDs has gained prominence owing to their eco-friendliness, cost-effectiveness, therapeutic properties, and abundant availability [22,23].

Despite the variety of materials available, ginger (Zingiber officinale) has gained popularity for several reasons. Firstly, ginger is rich in bioactive compounds such as gingerol, shogaol, paradol, and zingerone, which not only facilitate the formation of CDs but also enhance their antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties [24]. These inherent properties of ginger can be effectively transferred to the CDs, making them highly functional for biomedical applications [25]. Secondly, ginger is widely available and easy to cultivate, ensuring a consistent and sustainable supply for large-scale synthesis. The synthesis process using ginger is typically simple and eco-friendly, involving hydrothermal or solvothermal methods that do not require harsh chemicals or high energy inputs [26]. Lastly, the biocompatibility and non-toxicity of ginger-derived CDs are particularly appealing for applications in health and medicine, where safety is paramount. Therefore, ginger stands out as an optimal choice for the green synthesis of CDs, combining efficacy, sustainability, and safety, while also adding value to a widely available and renewable resource.

Thus, our study focuses on synthesizing CDs derived from ginger juice and evaluating their biomedical applications. These CDs were systematically tested for their ability to neutralize free radicals, combat various oral pathogens, and modulate inflammatory responses. This research seeks to provide innovative solutions for periodontal therapy and broader oral healthcare, addressing current treatment limitations and advancing nanomedicine.

Ginger rhizomes were purchased from local market of Sangli (16°51′11″N 74°34′59″E), Maharashtra, India. NaOH, HCl, NaNO2, ethyl alcohol, petroleum ether, H2SO4, trichloroacetic acid, ascorbic acid, were all procured from Quligen Pvt. Ltd. DPPH, nutrient agar, streptomycin, resazurin, DMEM medium, and MTT reagent were obtained from Himedia Pvt. Ltd. India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis and characterization of CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The ginger rhizomes were finely chopped and cleansed with distilled water. Subsequently, they were dried in a controlled laboratory oven at 50°C for 4 hours and ground into a fine powder using a grinder. To optimize the synthesis of CDs, three distinct samples were prepared: A) 3 gm ginger paste in 30 mL distilled water, B) 3 gm ginger powder in 30 mL distilled water, and C) 30 mL concentrated ginger juice.

In brief, the synthesis of CDs involved introducing samples A, B, and C into a Teflon-lined autoclave within a stainless-steel hydrothermal reactor. The mixtures were then agitated using a magnetic stirrer for a duration of 2 h. Subsequently, the resulting dispersion was transferred into the Teflon-lined autoclave within the stainless-steel hydrothermal reactor and subjected to a hydrothermal process, which entails the synthesis of CDs from ginger powder, for a duration of 8 h at 160°C in a laboratory oven. Following the hydrothermal procedure, the solution was filtered through 0.22 µm Whatman filter paper. Post-filtration, the solution underwent bath sonication for 25 minutes at 30°C to achieve a homogeneous dispersion of CDs.

The as-synthesized CD solutions were dispersed in deionized distilled water and exposed to various wavelengths in a UV chamber for fluorescence analysis. In the visible region, the bare CD solution appeared faint orange (Sample-A). Furthermore, exposure to bare green CDs solution at 254 nm (Sample-B) light revealed a faint green color, whereas under 365 nm (Sample-C) UV light, it exhibited blueish fluorescence.

To purify the CDs, the hydrothermally processed sample C was subjected to dialysis for 24 h using a dialysis bag (0.22 μm, 12,000 kDa-Hi media), facilitating the removal of insoluble carbon compounds. Subsequently, the completed dialyzed solution was centrifuged for 20 minutes at 8000 rpm (4°C to 25°C) using a refrigerated centrifuge (Elteck Overseas Pvt. India) to eliminate contaminants. The pH of the solution was adjusted using a phosphate buffer solution with a pH of 7.4. From this solution, 2 mL of ginger CDs solution was pipetted out and diluted with 3 mL of distilled water [27].
The preliminary particle size analysis for the CDs was performed using the instrument HORIBA SZ-100 involving the advantageous non-invasive back scatter detection at 173° [28].

The absorption spectrum of the prepared CDs underwent systematic examination across a wavelength range from 200 to 600 nm with double distilled water as a blank solution. Optical characteristics of the carbon dots were assessed using UV–Vis spectroscopy, employing the UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV 1800 Shimadzu, Japan). This spectroscopic technique operates on the fundamental principle of the interaction between chemical compounds and ultraviolet or visible light. Upon absorption of light by the material, it undergoes excitation and subsequent de-excitation processes, resulting in the generation of distinct and characteristic spectra [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR and measurement of zeta potential
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectrum of the CDs was acquired in KBr pellets on a FTIR spectrophotometer (Bruker ALPHA II, USA) in the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1 with a resolution of 2 cm−1. In brief, one drop of CDs was added to the surface of a KBr pellet, and excess CDs were removed using a capillary tube. Finally, FTIR analysis was performed on the dried pellets [30].

The zeta potential of the CDs was determined after fresh extraction using Horiba Scientific, SZ-100. Each sample was diluted with Dimethoxy sulfoxide (DMSO) and the electrophoretic mobility determined at 25°C and dispersant dielectric constant of 78.5. The obtained electrophoretic mobility values were used to calculate the zeta potential using the software Horiba Version 2.40 [31].

The morphology of the CDs was analyzed using a Scanning Electron Microscope (Carl Zeiss, Supra-55, Germany). For this analysis, 1 mg of the sample was dispersed in 10 mL of distilled water to prepare a suspension solution. A drop of the suspension was mounted on carbon tape using a micropipette and allowed to air dry before imaging at 20,000X magnification.

The sizes of the carbon dots were measured by HR-TEM (FEI, Tecnai T20- USA) with 200 KV. The particle sizes were statistically measured using image J software. For analysis, 1 mg of sample was dispersed in 1 mL of distilled water and dropped on to the copper grid using a micropipette. Then, it was allowed to air dry and placed in sample holder for imaging [32].

The nature of the powder materials was recorded on a powder X-Ray XRD Using Shimadzu, Model: XRD 600 using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å), monochromator, 40 KV current with scan speed of 10000 deg/minutes and scan range between 1,00,000- 9,00,000 degree Which is rapid and non-destructive analytical technique. The powder samples were placed on the sample holders and then subjected for analysis and data obtained were plotted using ORIGIN Ver.8.5 [33].

AFM analyses were performed using a Multimode 8 Bruker AFM microscope coupled with a Nanoscope V controller and a commercial silicon tip (RTESPA300; resonance of 300 kHz; nominal elastic constant of 40 N/m) in ScanAsyst™ air mode with a scan size of 3 µm. The samples were prepared by depositing 20 µL of the solutions onto a silicon wafer and drying them in an oven at 40°C [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening of biomedical properties of CDs
Antioxidant potential by DPPH free radical scavenging activity
ce:section.ce:para:
The antioxidant activity of the CDs was evaluated by measuring its free radical scavenging capability using DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) radicals. Various concentrations of the CDs (10, 20, 30 40, and 50 µL/mL), along with a standard (ascorbic acid) were prepared in separate test tubes. To each sample, 1.5 mL of 0.1 % methanolic DPPH solution was added. The mixtures were then incubated in the dark for 30 minutes. Following incubation, the samples were monitored for a color change from purple to yellow or red. Absorbance was measured at 510 nm using a Labtronics LT-114 colorimeter [35]. The radical scavenging activity was calculated using the following formula:
(1) DPPH radical scavenging activity (%) = [(Absorbance of control - Absorbance of test sample) / (Absorbance of control)] × 100

In brief, 100 μL of nitro-blue tetrazolium (NBT, 156 μM), 50 μL of reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH, 468 μM) and 100 μL of CDs and ascorbic acid of different concentrations (20, 40, 60, 80, 100 μL/mL) were mixed. The reaction was initiated by adding 10 μL of phenazine methosulphate (PMS, 60 μM). The reaction mixture was incubated at 25 °C for 5 minutes, followed by measurement of absorbance at 560 nm. The percentage inhibition was calculated by using the formula:
(2) Percentage inhibition = (Control–test/absorbance of control) × 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-Inflammatory Potential by Protein Denaturation Assay
ce:section.ce:para:
The reaction mixture (10 mL) was composed of 0.4 mL of egg albumin (sourced from fresh hen's eggs), 5.6 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 6.4), and 100 µL of varying concentrations (10–50 µg/mL) of CDs, and the standard (Diclofenac sodium). A similar volume of double-distilled water was used as the control. The mixtures were then incubated at 37 ± 2°C for 15 minutes, followed by heating at 70°C for 5 minutes. After cooling, the absorbance was measured at 660 nm, with distilled water serving as the blank [36]. The percentage inhibition of protein denaturation was calculated using the following formula:
(3) %Inhibition = C–T/C × 100
(Where, T= absorbance of CDs, C= absorbance of control).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity against selected oral pathogens by well diffusion method
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity by well-diffusion method for gram positive [Staphylococcus aureus, (NCIM 2654) Bacillus subtilis (ATCC 700396)] and gram negative [Escherichia coli (NCIM 2832), and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC 27853)] oral organisms was assessed for CDs solution. The inoculum for the microorganism was prepared using bacterial cultures. Fifteen milliliters of nutrient agar (Hi Media) medium was poured into sterilized Petri plates and allowed to solidify. A 100 µL sample of the bacterial suspension was pipetted and uniformly spread over the medium using a spreading rod until it dried thoroughly. Wells with a diameter of 6 mm were created using a sterile cork borer. Solutions of CDs, Streptomycin (as the standard), and CHX (positive control) at a concentration of 1 mg/mL were prepared in DMSO, and then added to the wells. The plates were incubated at 37°C for 24 hours. Streptomycin (1 mg/mL) was used as the positive control, and DMSO served as the negative control. Antibacterial activity was assessed by measuring the zones of inhibition (ZI). All experiments were conducted in triplicates [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifungal activity against selected oral fungi
ce:section.ce:para: The antifungal activity of the CDs solution was assessed using the well diffusion method against two significant oral fungal pathogens: Candida albicans and Aspergillus niger. The inoculum for the microorganisms was prepared from the fungal cultures. Fifteen milliliters of Sabouraud agar (HiMedia) medium were poured into sterilized Petri plates and allowed to cool and solidify. A 100 µL fungal suspension was pipetted out and spread evenly over the medium using a spreading rod until it dried properly. Wells with a 6 mm diameter were bored using a sterile cork borer. NSEO, the standard (Miconazole), and a positive control (100 µg/mL) were prepared in DMSO, and 100 µL of each test solution was added to the wells. The Petri plates were then incubated at 37°C for 24 h. Miconazole (1 mg/mL) was used as the positive control, and DMSO served as the negative control. Antifungal activity was evaluated by measuring the diameters of the zones of inhibition (ZI). All determinations were performed in triplicate [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial Activity of CDs Against Periodontal Pathogens
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of the CDs solution against selected periodontopathic organisms, including Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans (ATCC-33384), Prevotella intermedia (ATCC-25611), Tannerella forsythia (ATCC-43037), and Porphyromonas gingivalis (ATCC-33277), was evaluated using the well diffusion method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC)
ce:section.ce:para: The MIC of CDs was evaluated using the resazurin assay method as described by Sarker et al. (2007) [38] and Valsalam et al. (2019a,b) [39,40]. A 270 mg amount of resazurin was dissolved in 40 mL of sterile distilled water to prepare the indicator solution, ensuring complete dissolution with a vortex mixer. The assay was conducted in a 96-well plate under aseptic conditions.

Firstly, 100 µL of various concentrations of CDs (7.8, 15.6, 31.2, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, 1000 µg/mL), along with standard and positive control (CHX 0.2 %), were added to the wells. Then, 50 µL of nutrient broth was added to each well, followed by dilution. To each well, 10 µL of the resazurin indicator solution was added, along with 10 µL of fungal or bacterial suspension.

CHX 0.2 % in the same concentrations served as the positive control. Plates were covered with cling film to prevent dehydration and incubated for 18–24 hours at 37°C. Color changes from blue to pink or colorless indicated positive results, whereas no color change indicated negative results. The lowest concentration at which a color change was observed was considered the MIC value. Absorbance was measured at 600 nm using an ELISA reader.

The percentage of inhibition was calculated using the formula:
(4) % of Inhibition=Control- Test/Control ×100

For MBC determination, 50 µL aliquots from wells showing no visible bacterial growth were seeded on BHI agar plates and incubated for 24 h at 37°C. The MBC endpoint was defined as the lowest concentration at which 99.9 % of the bacterial population was killed, indicated by the absence of bacterial growth on the agar plates post-incubation [41].

The human gingival fibroblast cell lines (hTERT Gingival Fibroblast CRL- 4061TM) were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM, Thermo Fisher Scientific) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Thermo Fisher Scientific) and a 1 % solution of Antibiotic-Antimycotic 100X (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Cell passaging was conducted using 0.25 % Trypsin-EDTA solution (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and Phosphate Buffered Saline without magnesium and calcium ions (PBS, Thermo Fisher Scientific). Cultures of HGF cell lines (hTERT Gingival Fibroblast CRL-4061TM) were maintained in 75 cm2 cell culture flasks at 37°C in a 5 % CO2 incubator, with medium renewal every two days. The cells were observed under a microscope every two days to monitor growth and were split when reaching approximately 100 % confluence. Cell concentrations were determined using a Thoma cell counting chamber.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular docking studies of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Molecular docking of CDs was carried out in AutoDock platform using PyRx tool [42–44]. We prepared the Pyridinic-N CD-ligand ChemDraw 20.0 (.cdx), Arguslab 4.0 (.mol), and BIOVIA (.pdb) software tools. The ligand structure was docked to the target protein’s active binding site. Glucansucrase (GSase) from Streptococcus mutans is one of the major factors in dental caries pathogenesis. Thus, we retrieved the 3D X-ray crystallography protein.pdb (PDB ID: 3AIB) from RCSB site (https://www.rcsb.org/structure/3aib). The retrieved protein was pre-assessed for energy minimization by deleting all chains except chain A. Water and the ligand structures were removed and the protein then saved in .pdbqt and utilized for docking studies.

Cells were cultured at a concentration of 1 × 104 cells/mL in culture medium for 24 hours at 37°C and 5 % CO2. Subsequently, cells were seeded at a concentration of 104 cells/well in 100 μL of culture medium in 96-well tissue culture grade microplates. Different concentrations of samples (µg/mL) were added to the wells, and control wells were treated with DMSO (0.2 % in PBS) and cell lines. All samples were incubated in triplicate. To assess cell viability after culture and compare it with control cell survival, control wells were maintained. Following a 24-hour incubation period at 37°C and 5 % CO2 in a CO2 incubator, the culture medium was aspirated, and 20 μL of MTT reagent (5 mg/mL in PBS) was added to each well. After a 4-hour incubation at 37°C in a CO2 incubator, the medium was removed, and 200 μL of DMSO was added to each well, followed by a 10-minute incubation at 37°C with aluminum foil wrapping. Triplicate samples were analyzed by measuring the absorbance of each well using a microplate reader at a wavelength of 550 nm.

The spectral analysis revealed a single peak at 280 nm, as shown in Fig. 1a. Typically, CDs exhibit absorption peaks below 300 nm, consistent with our findings. Under visible light, the CDs solution manifested a faint orange hue (Sample A). Exposure to 254 nm UV light (Sample B) induced a faint green fluorescence, whereas illumination at 365 nm UV light (Sample C) produced a distinct bluish fluorescence, as depicted in Fig S1.

The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1e) analysis provided detailed insights into the chemical composition of the synthesized CDs. Several distinct peaks were observed, each corresponding to specific functional groups present in the CDs solution. A wide absorption peak at about 3400 cm−1, attributed to the extending vibrations of O–H bonds. The sharp peak at 1644 cm−1 signified the CO stretch of a secondary amide. Another prominent peak at 1514.49 cm−1 corresponded to the in-plane N–H bending vibration of the secondary amide. The NH stretching vibrations intramolecularly bonded to other molecules appeared at 3336 cm−1, followed by the stretching vibrations of -C–H around 2923 cm−1. Peak observed at 3008 cm−1 was characteristic of aromatic -C-H stretching vibrations. Additionally, the strong peak at 1161 cm−1 suggested the presence of C-O stretching vibrations in primary carboxylic acids, while the peak at 1099 cm−1 indicated CC bending of di-substituted groups.

The zeta potential of the CDs solution was measured to be −24.9 mV, indicating stability and dispersion. The negative zeta potential suggests the presence of negatively charged capping agents on the CDs surface (Fig. 1c).

The average particle size observed was 2.9 nm (Fig. 1d), confirming the presence of CDs in the solution. Additionally, the spectrum indicates that the CDs solution exhibits good dispersion properties.

The crystalline form of the compound was inferred based on the width of the diffraction peaks. XRD patterns of the synthesized CDs are illustrated in (Fig. 1b). Wide diffraction peaks observed around 2θ—20° indicate the presence of CDs. The spectrum depicts the XRD pattern, with significant peaks evident in the graph, suggesting the formation of the nanostructure. The breadth of the (111) peak, appearing at an angle of 32° on the 2θ scale, and another perspective (022), appearing at an angle of 27 on the 2θ scale, were utilized for analysis.

SEM provides insights into the particle size, size distribution, and morphology of CDs. Fig. 2a-d depict the morphological behavior of CDs, revealing a crystalline structure with lattice spacing distances of approximately 1–20 nm.

HR-TEM, in conjunction with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, was employed to investigate the crystalline structure, size, and shape of the synthesized CDs. In Fig. 3a, CDs are observed to align along one side of the ribbon-like stem. A high-resolution TEM image with a magnification scale of 10 nm, depicted in Fig. 3b, showcases nano combs with a lattice spacing of 4.2 nm, representing d-spacing of crystal planes of round-shaped CDs. Additionally, HR-TEM images at a magnification scale of 5 nm, along with the SAED pattern of a branch of CDs, as illustrated in Fig. 3C, provide crucial information about the CD's crystal structure. (Fig. 3a – Fig. 3d collectively indicate that the size of CDs ranged from 2 nm to 8 nm. Notably, the high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 3b highlights a typical dot, characterized by a round-shaped nanodot with a diameter of approximately 4 nm.

AFM imaging of CDs yields two-dimensional (2D) images derived from the random height counting of particles, while also offering three-dimensional (3D) insights into surface morphology. Fig. 3e and Fig. 3f present both 2D and 3D topographic AFM images of CDs, demonstrating the interaction between the AFM cantilever tip and the CDs under scrutiny.

To illustrate the three-dimensional morphology of CDs, Fig. 3e displays an AFM image of the dots. The investigated CDs exhibited a diameter of 10 nm, consistent with the AFM image depicted in Fig. 3e. Notably, the nanodots varied in height from 2.8 nm to 4.3 nm, representing several atomic layers. This suggests that the CDs are structured as round disks with a diameter ranging from 8 to 15 nm and a height consisting of multiple atomic layers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening of biomedical properties of CDs
Antioxidant potential of CDs by DPPH radical scavenging and SOD enzymatic assay
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were assessed for their antioxidant potential using the DPPH method across five different concentrations ranging from 10 to 50 µg/mL. At 50 µg/mL, CDs exhibited moderate antioxidant activity, with an inhibition rate of 21.16 %, compared to the standard Ascorbic acid, which showed 43 % inhibition at the same concentration ( Fig. 4a). Statistical analysis conducted through two-way ANOVA concerning absorbance values is detailed in Fig. 4b.

In the SOD enzymatic assay, CDs demonstrated the highest inhibition percentage at 1000 µg/mL, amounting to 50.93 %, compared to the standard Curcumin, which showed 72.65 % inhibition. However, as the concentration of CDs increased, the inhibition percentage displayed a dose-dependent increase (Fig. 4c). Statistical analysis via two-way ANOVA showed significant difference (p<0.0001) between standard and CDs (Fig. 4d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-inflammatory potential of CDs by protein denaturation assay
ce:section.ce:para: The inhibitory effect of CDs at various concentrations on protein denaturation is depicted in Fig. 4e. CDs and the standard Diclofenac sodium, at different concentrations (200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 µg/mL), exhibited inhibition of albumin denaturation in a concentration-dependent manner. CDs demonstrated moderate inhibition, reaching 32.27 % at 1000 µg/mL, compared to the standard drug Diclofenac sodium, which displayed 72.15 % inhibition at the same concentration (Fig. 4f). The statistical analysis showed significant differences between the Standard and CDs samples (p < 0.0001). This suggests that there is a notable distinction in the absorbance values between the two samples, with the standard sample demonstrating a statistically significant variance compared to the CDs sample (Fig. 4f).

The antibacterial activity of CDs was assessed through the well diffusion method, measuring the inhibition zones against selected oral bacterial strains. CDs demonstrated moderate activity against oral pathogens, with varying levels of inhibition observed. Specifically, CDs exhibited inhibition against Staphylococcus aureus, Lactobacillus acidophilus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Escherichia coli, with Pseudomonas aeruginosa showing the highest inhibition zone (8.0 ± 1.15 mm; Fig. 5a), followed by Escherichia coli (7.6 ± 0.57 mm; Fig. 5c), Staphylococcus aureus (7.3 ± 1.5 mm; Fig. 5d), and Lactobacillus acidophilus (7.0 ± 1.0 mm; Fig. 5b). These results were compared to the standard Streptomycin and distilled water, used as a negative control. Standard exhibited statistically significant results compared to CDs for all the oral pathogens tested.

The in-vitro antifungal activity of CDs against Candida albicans and Aspergillus niger was assessed using the well diffusion method. The results indicated promising outcomes, with CDs demonstrating a higher zone of inhibition in diameter (mm) against both fungal organisms. These findings are visually represented in Fig. 5e and Fig. 5 f. In both cases, the p-values were less than 0.05, suggesting that the differences in antifungal activity between the standard and CDs groups were statistically significant.

The antibacterial efficacy of CDs against periodontal pathogens, namely A. actinomycetemcomitans, P. gingivalis, T. forsythia, and P. intermedia, was evaluated using the well diffusion method. The observed zones of inhibition were measured as follows: A. actinomycetemcomitans (15.3 ± 1.5 mm; Fig. 6a), T. forsythia (15.03 ± 0.57 mm; Fig. 6b), P. gingivalis (11.06 ± 1.15 mm; Fig. 6c) and P. intermedia (14.6 ± 1.15 mm; Fig. 6d). These values were compared with those of Metronidazole (standard) and distilled water as a negative control. The standard antibiotic demonstrated considerable efficacy, yielding mean inhibition zones of 27.6 ± 2.3 mm for A. actinomycetemcomitans, 27.0 ± 1.0 mm for P. gingivalis, 28.6 ± 0.57 mm for T. forsythia, and 26.33 ± 2.52 mm for P. intermedia. The results of the zone of inhibition assay demonstrated significant differences between the standard antibiotics and CDs in their efficacy against the tested bacterial strains.

The MIC of CDs was determined using the resazurin method, as shown in Fig. 6e – Fig. 6h. The results indicate that CDs inhibited the growth of periodontal pathogens (A. actinomycetemcomitans, T. forsythia, P. gingivalis, P. intermedia) at concentrations ranging from 250 µg/mL to 500 µg/mL. Specifically, CDs exhibited MIC values of 500 µg/mL for A. actinomycetemcomitans, P. gingivalis, and T. forsythia, while a lower MIC of 250 µg/mL was observed for P. intermedia. These findings suggest that the MIC of CDs varied across different bacterial strains. Statistical analysis using two-way ANOVA, revealed significant differences between the standard antibiotic and CDs at various concentrations for different bacterial strains. Specifically, significant differences were observed for P. gingivalis at concentrations except 7.8, 31.2, and 62.5 µg/L, for A. actinomycetemcomitans at concentrations 125, 250, 500, and 1000 µg/mL, for T. forsythia at concentrations 15.6, 125, 250, 500, and 1000 µg/mL, and for P. intermedia at concentrations 15.6, 31.2, 62.5, 125, 250, and 500 µg/mL
The MBC of CDs against tested periodontal pathogens (was found to be 500 µg/mL. In comparison, the standard drug Metronidazole exhibited an MBC of <7.86 µg/mL ( Fig. 7a – Fig. 7D).

Targeting GSase with specific inhibitors like CDs is crucial for disrupting the biofilm formation and reducing the pathogenicity of Streptococcus mutans. The molecular docking was conducted mainly to recognize the binding affinity of CDs. In the results, the molecular mechanistic values obtained were remarkable. Table 1 depicts the binding affinity values (kcal/mol) of nine different orientations. At a lowest binding energy value of −13.6 kcal/mol the Ligand-Receptor complex found to be stable with a RMSD of 0. RMSD (Root Mean Square Deviation) is the most commonly used quantitative measure of the similarity between two superimposed atomic coordinates. Fig. 8a representing the corresponding orientation of the 0 RMSD Ligand-Receptor complex.

We assessed the key residue binding between the Ligand-Receptor complex and also assessed the mode of interaction. Table 2 depicts the non-bonded interaction established between CD-GSase. The major expected interactions such as conventional hydrogen bond (CHB), carbon hydrogen bond (CarHB) and non-covalent bonds such as pi-pi and cation-pi interactions were widely established. A typical image of the comparison between the binding mechanism of the ligand (2-(N-Morpholino)-ethanesulfonic acid (MES)) and CD docked to the protein is shown in Figs. 8b-8e. The common key residues Thr426 and Ile427 in both ligand interactions indicates that the CDs exactly binding the active binging site. The clusters of binding interactions are more advantage between CD-GSase. Conclusively, this molecular docking studies ensures the docking between CD-GSase gives more in-sight in to the potential of treating dental caries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation of CDs over HGF cell lines
ce:section.ce:para: In the cytotoxicity evaluation of CDs over normal human gingival fibroblast cell lines (hTERT Gingival Fibroblast CRL-4061TM), CDs exhibited 11.50 % percentage of inhibition while positive control shown 8.12 % inhibition of cells. These results suggest that CDs may not cause harm to normal gingival fibroblast cells, as indicated by the percentage of viability (Fig. 7e – Fig. 7i).

The CDs were successfully synthesized using ginger as a precursor, demonstrating an environmentally friendly approach to nanomaterial production. Utilizing ginger, a natural and renewable resource, minimized the reliance on toxic chemicals and reduced environmental impact. This method leveraged the abundant and biodegradable nature of ginger, aligning with sustainable development goals by promoting green chemistry principles.

The UV-Vis spectral analysis of the synthesized CDs revealed a single prominent absorption peak at 280 nm. This absorption peak is characteristic of π-π* transitions within aromatic CC bonds, which is a typical feature for carbon-based nanomaterials [45]. The UV–visible spectrum of CDs typically exhibits an optical absorption band between 250 and 350 nm. This band is attributed to the excitation of pi-electrons (π → π*) within the aromatic π system [46]. The observed peak at 280 nm aligns well with the expected behavior of CDs, which generally exhibit strong absorption in the UV region, particularly below 300 nm. The tailing band observed at 300 nm could be attributed to the n–π* transition of oxygen-containing functional groups [46].

This absorption behavior is attributed to the quantum confinement effects and the presence of conjugated π-domains within the carbon structure. The peak's position and intensity provide insight into the electronic structure and surface states of the CDs, confirming their successful synthesis and potential applicability in optoelectronic and bioimaging applications due to their strong UV absorption properties. Naik et al., synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) via hydrothermal synthesis using carbon powder and ammonia. They observed a UV–VIS peak around 278 nm, attributing it to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains in the carbon core [47].

CDs refer to small, nanometer-sized fluorescent carbon materials exhibiting photoluminescent (PL) properties [48]. The fluorescence profile of the CDs indicated their ability to emit light under UV exposure. When dispersed in double distilled water, the CDs exhibited different colors upon exposure to different UV wavelengths: faint orange under visible light, faint green under 254 nm UV light, and bluish fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. This variation in fluorescence under different wavelengths suggests the presence of diverse surface states and functional groups on the CDs, which can influence their optical properties. The distinct fluorescence emission makes CDs suitable for applications in bioimaging, where different emission wavelengths can be used for multi-color labeling. A study by Das et al., revealed that the as-synthesized CDs exhibited a bright blue fluorescence when illuminated with UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm, which was easily observable in diluted colloidal solutions. These findings are comparable with our results, where our synthesized CDs also demonstrated similar blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. This consistency underscores the effective photoluminescent properties of CDs synthesized using environmentally friendly methods [46].

The FT-IR spectral analysis offered intricate insights into the chemical composition of the synthesized CDs. The carbon framework of CDs often bears various functional groups due to distinct hybridizations [49]. The prominent peak observed at 1644 cm⁻¹ denoted the CO stretching of a secondary amide. Characteristic of amides, this peak commonly resides within the 1680–1630 cm⁻¹ range, with an accompanying peak around 1540 cm⁻¹, as noted here at 1515 cm⁻¹. This secondary amide feature represents the in-plane N–H bending vibration and typically manifests between 1570 and 1515 cm⁻¹ [50]. These findings resonate with those of Ferjani et al., (2024), who identified analogous functional groups in CDs synthesized from avocado peels, emphasizing the diverse and intricate surface chemistry of CDs. Their study observed a strong peak at approximately 1635 cm⁻¹, indicative of the CO stretch of a secondary amide, along with a corresponding peak at 1534 cm⁻¹, signifying the in-plane N–H bending vibration of the secondary amide [51]. This rich surface chemistry enhances the potential of CDs for various chemical modifications and interactions, making them suitable for a wide range of biological and environmental applications.

Zeta potential is a critical parameter for assessing the colloidal stability of nanoparticles; values greater than +30 mV or less than −30 mV typically indicate strong repulsion between particles, thus preventing aggregation [52]. The zeta potential measurement of −24.9 mV for the synthesized CDs is a significant indicator of their stability and dispersion in aqueous solutions. While the zeta potential of −24.9 mV is slightly lower than the threshold for optimal stability, it still suggests that the CDs are reasonably stable due to the presence of negatively charged functional groups on their surface. These groups likely originate from the capping agents used during synthesis, which enhance the electrostatic repulsion between particles, thus contributing to their stability. Stable CDs can efficiently circulate in biological systems without aggregating, ensuring that the encapsulated drugs are delivered effectively to target sites. Moreover, the negative surface charge can influence the interaction of CDs with cell membranes and other biological structures, potentially enhancing their efficacy in biomedical applications. Comparatively, in the literature, similar stability trends have been observed. For instance, Basha et al., (2024) reported that CDs with a zeta potential of around − 9.9 eV exhibited good stability and dispersibility in aqueous solutions, which is comparable to the results obtained in this study [53]. Additionally, Sachdev et al., (2015) demonstrated that CDs with zeta potentials of −24.9 mV were stable and effective for biomedical applications, reinforcing the relevance of these findings [54].

DLS analysis revealed an average particle size of 2.9 nm for the synthesized CDs, confirming their nanoscale dimensions. This small size is particularly advantageous for applications requiring uniform distribution, such as drug delivery systems and imaging agents [55]. The nanoscale size ensures efficient cellular uptake, minimal interference with biological processes, and improved penetration into tissues [56]. Reportedly, CDs with sizes below 10 nm exhibit superior cellular uptake and bio-distribution properties compared to larger nanoparticles [57,58]. This is because smaller particles can more easily navigate the intracellular environment and cross biological barriers, enhancing their potential in biomedical applications. Furthermore, CDs with a narrow size distribution exhibit more consistent photoluminescent properties and better performance in bioimaging, underscoring the importance of size uniformity [59].

The XRD patterns of the CDs displayed broad diffraction peaks around 2θ ≈ 20°, indicating an amorphous structure with some crystalline domains. In CDs, the identification peaks corresponding to (111) and (022) planes through X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns suggests that the CDs possess regions of ordered, crystalline structure within an otherwise amorphous matrix. The (111) and (022) planes, in particular, can influence the material's electronic, optical, and mechanical properties, making them relevant for applications in sensing, imaging, and electronic devices. The (111) plane has been observed previously in carbon dots, indicating a level of crystallinity within these nanomaterials [60,61].

The SEM images of the synthesized CDs revealed crystalline structures with lattice spacing distances ranging from 1 to 20 nm. This observation was further corroborated by HR-TEM images, which confirmed the presence of round-shaped nanodots with diameters around 4 nm and lattice spacings of 4.2 nm. The Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern also supported the crystalline nature of the CDs. The findings are consistent with prior research by Zhang et al., who demonstrated through TEM analysis that particle sizes of synthesized CDs ranged between 2 and 5 nm [62]. The TEM images of chiral CDs synthesized by Zhang et al., showed that the particles were well-dispersed and uniform, with diameters ranging from 4 to 8 nm [63]. These findings underscore the consistency in particle size and morphology, which is crucial for their application in various fields such as bioimaging and drug delivery in dentistry.

AFM serves as a powerful tool for determining the thickness and topographic profile of particles, offering valuable insights into their morphology and topology [64]. The AFM analysis revealed that the CDs synthesized from ginger had diameters of 10 nm and heights ranging from 2.8 to 4.3 nm, consistent with several atomic layers. This morphological consistency aligns with previous findings by Zhang et al., who observed similar heights for L-CDs (2.8 nm) and D-CDs (2.7 nm) [62]. The agreement in these measurements suggests that the synthesis method is robust and reproducible, yielding CDs with uniform dimensions. The use of ginger as a precursor likely contributes to this uniformity due to its consistent chemical composition and the controlled synthesis conditions.

The prevalence of free radicals, driven by oxidative stress or a deficit in antioxidants, has been implicated in the pathogenesis of periodontal disease [65]. Sufficient levels of antioxidants are crucial for protecting periodontal tissues from damage induced by reactive oxygen species [66]. Recent research has underscored the growing significance of antioxidants in both the therapeutic management of periodontal disease and the preservation of periodontal health [67]. Numerous investigations have solidified the efficacy of CDs as promising antioxidants. For instance, Murru et al., conducted a study wherein CDs were derived from vegetable waste materials such as tea and grapes via a hydrothermal method. The resulting CDs showcased exceptional antioxidant properties [68]. Similarly, Watcharamongkol et al., synthesized CDs from cassava pulps utilizing hydrothermal techniques and evaluated their effectiveness against the DPPH radical. The CDs derived from cassava pulps exhibited remarkable scavenging capabilities [69]. The capacity of CDs to furnish H+ ions stems from the presence of surface functional groups like -COOH, -OH, and -NH2 [20]. Their antioxidant prowess was moderately demonstrated, evidenced by a 21.16 % inhibition at a concentration of 50 µg/mL in the DPPH assay and a 50.93 % inhibition at 100 µg/mL in the SOD assay. The underlying reason may lie in the environmentally friendly nature of the green synthesis method, which preserves the bioactivity of ginger's constituents, facilitating their seamless integration into the carbon dots. Moreover, this efficacy may be attributed to the intriguing dual response some carbon nanomaterials exhibit towards reactive species; they can either scavenge them, thereby diminishing the population of free radicals and Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS), or autonomously generate reactive species under irradiation. Such a dualistic behavior is absent in singular molecule antioxidants like ascorbic acid [70]. Nevertheless, while these values fall short compared to standard antioxidants such as ascorbic acid and curcumin, they still underscore the intrinsic antioxidant attributes of CDs. Generally, CDs perform suboptimally when benchmarked against standard antioxidants like ascorbic acid or Vitamin E. This disparity is also linked to the inherent challenges in synthesis; achieving precise modulation of the surface, which dictates antioxidant properties, remains a formidable task [70]. Furthermore, the antioxidant properties of carbon dots CDs are intricately shaped by a multitude of factors such as the configuration of sp2 hybrid carbon domains, the propensity to donate hydrogen, mechanisms governing electron transport, the presence of unpaired electrons arising from vacancies and defects, the nature of elements used for doping, and the composition of surface functional groups [71].

Periodontal diseases, involving inflammation of the gingiva, bone, and periodontal ligament, can lead to tooth loss and heighten systemic inflammation [72]. Thus, anti-inflammatory drugs are crucial in the management of periodontitis due to their ability to mitigate the inflammation that is central to the disease's pathogenesis. CDs have been previously explored as an effective anti-inflammatory treatment for periodontitis [73]. CDs demonstrated a moderate level of anti-inflammatory activity, exhibiting a 32.27 % inhibition of protein denaturation at a concentration of 50 µg/mL, in contrast to the 40.5 % inhibition observed with diclofenac sodium. This indicates the potential of CDs to mitigate inflammation, rendering them promising candidates for the development of anti-inflammatory therapies. The utilization of a green synthesis method involving ginger could contribute to the observed anti-inflammatory activity of CDs. Ginger is renowned for its bioactive compounds, including gingerols and shogaols, which possess anti-inflammatory properties. During the synthesis process, these beneficial compounds may be preserved or incorporated into the structure of the CDs, thereby endowing them with anti-inflammatory attributes. Furthermore, this could be assigned to fact that ginger-CDs may inhibit pro-inflammatory cytokine levels and escalate anti-inflammatory cytokine expression [74,75].

The primary etiological factor in the initiation and progression of periodontal disease is dental plaque biofilm, an organized consortium of microorganisms embedded within a complex intercellular matrix [76]. Thus, effective antibacterial treatment helps to eliminate or reduce the bacterial load, thereby mitigating inflammation, preventing further tissue destruction, and promoting periodontal healing and stability. In our study, the moderate antibacterial activity displayed by CDs against oral bacteria, along with their promising antifungal activity against Candida albicans and Aspergillus niger, suggests their potential as antimicrobial agents. The observed zones of inhibition against periodontal pathogens and the minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of 500 µg/mL for most bacteria and 250 µg/mL for P. intermedia suggest that CDs hold promise as antibacterial agents for periodontal infections. The observed antimicrobial activity of CDs, although lower than that of standard antibiotics and antifungals, could have stemmed from their capability to generate ROS when photoexcited. These ROS might then have penetrated the bacterial cell wall and membrane, potentially causing oxidative stress and damage to essential biomolecules such as DNA, RNA, and proteins. Consequently, this oxidative damage might have disrupted vital cellular processes and led to intracellular protein inactivation, lipid peroxidation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and eventual cell membrane disintegration, possibly resulting in cell death through necrosis or apoptosis [77,78].

Several factors could contribute to the comparatively lower effectiveness of CDs against bacteria and fungi. Firstly, the mechanism of action of CDs might differ from traditional antibiotics and antifungals. CDs may exert their antimicrobial activity through physical interactions, such as membrane disruption or oxidative stress induction, rather than specific molecular targets. This difference in mode of action could result in varied potency against different microorganisms.

Additionally, the surface properties and functional groups of CDs play a crucial role in determining their antimicrobial activity. The synthesis method, including the precursor material and reaction conditions, influences the composition and surface chemistry of CDs, which in turn affect their interactions with microorganisms. CDs synthesized using a green approach with ginger, for example, may incorporate bioactive compounds with antimicrobial properties, enhancing their efficacy against certain pathogens.

Dental caries is a significant global health issue, primarily driven by the biofilm-forming abilities of Streptococcus mutans. The production of glucans by glucansucrase (GSase) is a key factor in this pathogen's pathogenicity, facilitating biofilm development and cariogenicity. Through molecular docking analysis, we demonstrated that CDs exhibit a significant binding affinity to GSase, suggesting their potential as effective antimicrobial agents.

The binding affinity values obtained indicate that CDs can form stable complexes with GSase, which is critical for their inhibitory function. The low binding energy associated with the strongest interaction suggests a robust affinity, providing a theoretical basis for their application in disrupting biofilm formation. This is particularly relevant, as S. mutans is notorious for its ability to form biofilms that contribute to dental caries.

In our analysis, we identified several important interactions between the CDs and GSase. The presence of conventional hydrogen bonds, carbon-hydrogen bonds, and various non-covalent interactions such as π-π and cation-π interactions highlights the complexity of the binding mechanism. These interactions not only contribute to the stability of the ligand-receptor complex but also indicate that the binding of CDs may effectively inhibit the enzymatic activity of GSase. The significant involvement of key residues in the active site reinforces the precision with which CDs can target this enzyme.

HGFs are essential for the regeneration and repair of periodontal tissues, including the gingiva and periodontal ligament, making it imperative that antibacterial treatments for periodontitis do not demonstrate cytotoxicity towards these cells [79]. The low cytotoxicity displayed by CDs towards normal human gingival fibroblast cell lines, with an inhibition percentage of only 11.50 %, suggests that they hold promise for safe utilization in biomedical applications, particularly those involving human cells. As per ISO 10993–5 standards, cell viability percentages surpassing 80 % are classified as indicative of non-cytotoxicity [80].

This result is significant for several reasons. Firstly, in biomedical applications where interaction with human cells is essential, such as drug delivery systems or tissue engineering, ensuring minimal cytotoxicity is paramount to prevent adverse effects on cellular viability and function. The low cytotoxicity of CDs towards human gingival fibroblast cells indicates a favorable biocompatibility profile, suggesting that they may not induce significant harm or cellular damage. Moreover, the low cytotoxicity of CDs implies that they may have a selective impact on targeted pathogens while sparing normal human cells. This selective cytotoxicity is advantageous in antimicrobial applications, as it allows for the eradication of pathogens while preserving the viability of host cells, thereby minimizing collateral damage and promoting tissue healing.

The favorable safety profile of CDs for biomedical applications can be attributed to their inherent properties, such as their small size, surface functionalization, and chemical composition. These factors influence their interactions with biological systems, including cellular uptake, intracellular trafficking, and potential toxicity mechanisms. Additionally, the synthesis method and purification techniques employed during CD production can impact their biocompatibility, emphasizing the importance of careful optimization and characterization for biomedical use.

The study presents a comprehensive analysis of CDs synthesized from ginger rhizomes through the hydrothermal method, highlighting their multifunctional potential in various biomedical applications. The CDs exhibited unique optical properties, particularly excitation-dependent emission, which makes them suitable for diverse imaging and diagnostic purposes. Structural characterization confirmed their nanoscale dimensions and consistent morphology, crucial for ensuring uniformity in their application. A pivotal aspect of the study was the assessment of the CDs' cytotoxicity using the MTT assay on human gingival fibroblast cell lines. The results indicated that the CDs were biocompatible even at higher concentrations, underscoring their safety for potential medical and therapeutic applications. This biocompatibility is essential for their use in clinical settings, ensuring that they do not induce adverse cellular responses. The CDs also demonstrated impressive free radical scavenging activity and total antioxidant capacity. These properties are significant as they suggest the CDs can mitigate oxidative stress, which is implicated in various pathological conditions. The ability to scavenge free radicals and exhibit strong antioxidant activity positions these CDs as potential agents in managing oxidative stress-related diseases. Moreover, the antimicrobial and antifungal properties of the CDs were notably effective against various bacterial and fungal strains. This includes their inhibitory effects on Porphyromonas gingivalis, a key pathogen in periodontitis. The ability of CDs to combat such antibiotic-resistant bacteria highlights their potential as alternative or adjunctive treatments in the fight against bacterial infections, particularly in oral health. Overall, the CDs synthesized from ginger rhizomes show considerable promise due to their biocompatibility, antioxidant capabilities, and antimicrobial activities. These multifunctional attributes suggest they could play a crucial role in addressing antibiotic resistance and enhancing oral health, potentially offering a novel therapeutic approach in the treatment of periodontitis and other related conditions. Future research on CDs should prioritize in vivo safety and efficacy studies, explore the mechanisms of biofilm disruption, and optimize synthesis for enhanced targeted delivery. Additionally, it is essential to investigate the broader implications of using CDs in dental applications, including long-term safety profiles and potential side effects. Gaining a comprehensive understanding of these aspects will be crucial for effectively translating laboratory findings into clinical practice.

Shaeesta Khaleelahmed Bhavikatti and Mohmed Isaqali Karobari conceptualized the experiment. Shaeesta Khaleelahmed Bhavikatti, Siti Lailatul Akmar Zainuddin and Mohmed Isaqali Karobari designed and supervised the experiments. Shaeesta Khaleelahmed Bhavikatti, Padma B. Dandge, and Masidd Khalate performed the experiments and measurements. Masidd Khalate and Sameer J. Nadaf conducted data analysis and validation. Shaeesta Khaleelahmed Bhavikatti and Rosmaliza Binti Ramli wrote the manuscript, and Siti Lailatul Akmar Zainuddin, Sameer J. Nadaf and Mohmed Isaqali Karobari revised it.
